text: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly widely used, and it is natural to wonder whether they can affordably represent some basic elements of geometry with their underlying spatial relationships. Using BERT and GPT-2, we studied representations of the shapes described in Well-Known Text (WKT) format as inputs to the subsequent classification and regression tasks to see how well LLMs describe the inherent properties of these shapes. Our experiments show that even though LLM-based shape representations are surprisingly good at describing shapes as defined by their type and identifying their spatial relationships (up to 73% accuracy), they severely deteriorate any quantitative values that might exist between tokenization and therefore suffer from imprecision when it comes to calculating area or centroids and identifying neighbouring shapes. This study reveals that there is a strong need to (i) incorporate domain-specific knowledge into foundation models and (ii) develop new tokenization techniques for affording the required GeoAI use cases with high precision.
explanation: The text is highly polished and uses generic research phrasing with few concrete methodological details or citations. This pattern is often seen in AI-generated abstracts, though a real paper could also resemble it.
